step.title: Step 4
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 14 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Game over triggered - my path led into a wall. The board has reset to the initial state. My BFS was wrong because I incorrectly identified walkable cells. Need to re-analyze the maze structure carefully.
- Plan: Re-examine the maze from the reset state, determine which cells are actually walkable, and find the correct path.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the maze clearly. Let me look at the structure more carefully. The maze has alternating white (W) and gray (g) 3x3 blocks with black (B) separators. 
Looking at the pattern, I think the issue is that the maze might be a checkerboard pattern where only ONE color (either white OR gray) is walkable, not both. Let me think about this...
Looking at the maze:
- Row 0: bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
- Row 1: BBB BBB ggg BBB ggg BBB BBB BBB ggg BBB BBB
- Row 2: BBB BBB WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW
- ...
Wait, I see the pattern now. The maze has:
- 3x3 blocks of color (W, g, b, N)
- 3x3 blocks of B as walls between them
- But some adjacent blocks share edges directly
Let me look more carefully. The pattern seems to be:
- Each "cell" in the maze is 3x3 pixels
- Cells are separated by 3-pixel wide black corridors in some places
Actually, looking at row 15: `BbbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWWBBBBBBB`
This is: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
So the cells are:
- bbb (blue, player) at col 15-17
- ggg at col 18-20
- WWW at col 21-23
- BBB at col 24-26 (wall)
- WWW at col 27-29
- ggg at col 30-32
- WWW at col 33-35
- ggg at col 36-38
- WWW at col 39-41
- BBB at col 42-44 (wall)
- BBB at col 45-47 (wall)
- BBB at col 48-50 (wall)
So the maze is NOT a regular grid! Some cells are separated by BBB walls, and some are adjacent. The walkable path goes through alternating W and g blocks, with BBB blocks acting as walls.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the maze is a graph where:
- Each 3x3 non-black block is a node
- Adjacency is determined by whether two blocks share an edge
- The player moves between adjacent blocks
But wait, I already tried BFS treating all non-B cells as walkable and it failed. The issue might be that the player can only move through blocks of a specific color, or there's a different movement mechanic.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the first successful move (RIGHT from start):
- Player was at blue block (15-17, 15-17)
- After RIGHT, player moved to... let me check the transition
Actually, the first RIGHT move was successful - the player moved from [16,17] to [16,23]. That's a move of 6 columns, which is 2 cells (each cell is 3 pixels). Wait, no - [16,17] to [16,23] is 6 columns, but the blue block is at cols 15-17 and the next block (ggg) is at cols 18-20, then WWW at 21-23. So the player moved from the blue block to the WWW block, skipping the ggg block.
Hmm, that's strange. Let me re-examine. The charcoal was at [16,17] which is the rightmost column of the blue block. After RIGHT, it moved to [16,23] which is the rightmost column of the WWW block at cols 21-23. So the player jumped over the ggg block!
Wait, maybe the player moves 2 cells at a time? Or maybe the maze is structured differently than I thought.
Let me look at the structure again:
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
If the player is at the blue block and moves RIGHT, it goes to the WWW block (skipping ggg). This suggests the player moves through corridors, not through the colored blocks themselves.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the maze structure. Maybe:
- The colored 3x3 blocks (W, g, b, N) are the walls/obstacles
- The black 3x3 blocks (B) are the corridors/path
- The player moves through the black corridors
But that doesn't make sense either because the player starts on a blue block...
Let me look at this differently. Maybe the maze is made of 6x6 "super-cells" where each super-cell contains a pattern of 3x3 blocks. Or maybe the movement is different.
Actually, let me look at the first move more carefully. The player was at [16,17] (within blue block at 15-17, 15-17). After RIGHT, the player was at [16,23]. The distance is 6 columns. Looking at the ascii:
- Col 15-17: bbb (blue)
- Col 18-20: ggg (gray)  
- Col 21-23: WWW (white)
So the player moved from the blue block to the white block, passing through the gray block. The player seems to move 2 cells (6 pixels) per action.
Wait, but that means the player moves 2 blocks per action. Let me re-examine the compressed grid with this understanding.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the maze structure is:
- Each "cell" is actually 6x6 pixels (two 3x3 blocks)
- The player moves one cell (6 pixels) per action
Let me re-build the grid with 6x6 cells:
Row 15-20, Col 15-47:
- Cell (0,0): rows 15-20, cols 15-20 → bbb/ggg in top 3 rows, BBB/ggg in bottom 3 rows
Hmm, this doesn't work cleanly either.
Let me try a different approach. Let me trace the actual path the player took in the first few moves to understand the movement pattern.
From the history:
1. Start: charcoal at [16,17]
2. After RIGHT: charcoal at [16,23] (moved +6 cols)
3. Then I did RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT...
The player moved 6 pixels per action. So each action moves the player 6 pixels in the given direction. This means the effective cell size is 6x6, not 3x3.
Let me rebuild the maze with 6x6 cells:
- Rows 15-47, that's 33 rows / 3 = 11 cells... no, 33/6 = 5.5, doesn't divide evenly.
Hmm, let me reconsider. The maze spans rows 15-47 and cols 15-47. That's 33 pixels in each direction. 33/3 = 11 cells. But the player moves 6 pixels per action, which is 2 cells.
So the player moves 2 cells per action. This means in the 11x11 compressed grid, each action moves the player 2 cells.
Let me re-examine my first BFS. The path was:
RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, ...
With 2-cell movement, RIGHT moves from col 0 to col 2, then RIGHT again moves to col 4, etc.
Wait, but I said the first RIGHT moved the player from the blue block at col 15-17 to the white block at col 21-23. That's from cell col 0 to cell col 2 in the 3x3 grid. So yes, each action moves 2 cells.
Let me rebuild the BFS with this understanding:
- Grid is 11x11 (3x3 pixel blocks)
- Each action moves 2 cells in the given direction
- Player starts at cell (0, 0)
- Goal is at cell (10, 10)
Let me check: from (0,0), RIGHT goes to (0,2), RIGHT again goes to (0,4), etc.
But wait, my first BFS assumed 1-cell movement. That's why the path was wrong! Let me redo the BFS with 2-cell movement.
Actually, let me verify this hypothesis first. Let me check what happened after the first few moves.
From the first batch of actions (RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN):
Starting from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0, 2)
- RIGHT → (0, 4)
- DOWN → (2, 4)
- DOWN → (4, 4)
- DOWN → (6, 4)
- DOWN → (8, 4)
- RIGHT → (8, 6)
- RIGHT → (8, 8)
- UP → (6, 8)
- UP → (4, 8)
- RIGHT → (4, 10)
- RIGHT → (4, 12) - out of bounds? Grid is 11x11, so col 12 is out of bounds.
Hmm, that would cause an issue. Let me check the grid again.
Actually wait, let me look at the grid more carefully:
Row 0: W g W B W g W g W B B
Row 1: B B g B g B B B g B B
Row 2: B B W B W g W g W g W
...
With 2-cell movement, from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0, 2) → W (walkable)
- RIGHT → (0, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (2, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (4, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (6, 4) → g (walkable)
- DOWN → (8, 4) → g (walkable)
- RIGHT → (8, 6) → g (walkable)
- RIGHT → (8, 8) → W (walkable)
- UP → (6, 8) → W (walkable)
- UP → (4, 8) → B (wall!)
That's the problem! At step 10 (UP from (6,8) to (4,8)), the cell is B (black/wall). So the path hit a wall.
But wait, the game didn't report game_over until much later. Let me re-check...
Actually, looking at the result: the game executed 35 actions but stopped early at action 14 with game_over. The executed actions were: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT.
Wait, that's from the SECOND batch of actions, not the first. The first batch executed all 35 actions without game_over. Let me re-read...
First batch result: executed 35 actions, no game_over, but level not completed. Player ended at [23, 40].
Second batch result: executed 14 actions, game_over at action 14.
So the first batch of 35 actions completed but didn't reach the goal. The player was at [23, 40] which is cell (6, 9) in the 3x3 grid (row (23-15)/3 = 2.67, hmm that doesn't work cleanly).
Let me recalculate. If the player moves 6 pixels per action (2 cells), and started at cell (0,0):
After 35 actions, the player would be at some position. Let me trace through...
Actually, I think the issue is more fundamental. Let me reconsider whether the player really moves 2 cells per action, or if my initial grid mapping was wrong.
Let me check the first move again:
- Start: charcoal at [16, 17]
- After RIGHT: charcoal at [16, 23]
- Difference: 6 columns
The blue block is at cols 15-17. After RIGHT, the player is at col 23. The block at cols 21-23 is WWW (white). So the player moved from the blue block to the white block.
But looking at row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
Col 15-17: bbb
Col 18-20: ggg
Col 21-23: WWW
So the player moved from col 15-17 to col 21-23, which is 2 cells (6 pixels) to the right. This confirms 2-cell movement.
But wait, maybe the movement isn't always 2 cells. Maybe it depends on the maze structure. Let me think about this differently.
What if the maze is actually a graph where nodes are the colored blocks and edges connect adjacent blocks? The player moves from one block to an adjacent block in the given direction.
Looking at the structure:
Row 15: bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
The blue block (bbb) at col 15-17 is adjacent to ggg at col 18-20, which is adjacent to WWW at col 21-23. So from blue, moving RIGHT would go to ggg, then to WWW.
But the player moved from blue directly to WWW, skipping ggg. This suggests the player moves 2 blocks per action.
OK let me just try a completely different approach. Let me build the grid with 6x6 cells and see if it makes more sense.
Actually, I realize the issue. The maze might not have uniform cell sizes. Let me look at the structure more carefully.
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
Row 18: B BBB BBB ggg BBB ggg BBB BBB BBB ggg BBB BBB
These rows have different patterns. Row 15 has colored blocks (bbb, ggg, WWW), while row 18 has mostly BBB with some ggg.
This suggests the maze has a checkerboard-like structure where some areas are walkable corridors and others are walls.
Let me try yet another approach: treat the maze as a graph where each connected non-black region is a node, and the player moves between adjacent nodes.
Actually, I think the simplest explanation is that the player moves 6 pixels (2 cells of 3x3) per action. Let me build the grid with this understanding and redo BFS.
Grid with 3x3 cells (11x11):
Row 0: b g W B W g W g W B B
Row 1: B B g B g B B B g B B
Row 2: B B W B W g W g W g W
Row 3: B B g B g B g B B B g
Row 4: B B W g W B W B B B W
Row 5: B B B B B B g B B B g
Row 6: W g W g W g W B W g W
Row 7: g B g B B B B B B B B
Row 8: W g W g W g W B W g W
Row 9: B B g B B B g B g B g
Row 10: W g W B B B W g W B N
With 2-cell movement, from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0,2): W ✓
- RIGHT → (0,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (2,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (4,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (6,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (8,4): W ✓
- RIGHT → (8,6): W ✓
- RIGHT → (8,8): W ✓
- UP → (6,8): W ✓
- UP → (4,8): B ✗ WALL!
So the 10th action (UP) would hit a wall. But the first batch executed 35 actions without game_over. This means either:
1. My grid is wrong
2. The player doesn't move 2 cells per action
3. The player can move through walls
Let me reconsider. Maybe the player moves 1 cell per action, and my initial grid mapping was wrong.
Let me check: if the player moves 1 cell (3 pixels) per action:
- Start at [16, 17] (within blue block at cols 15-17)
- After RIGHT: [16, 23] (within white block at cols 21-23)
- That's a move of 6 pixels, not 3.
Unless... the player moves to the CENTER of the next cell. The blue block is at cols 15-17, center is col 16. The white block is at cols 21-23, center is col 22. But the player was at col 17 and moved to col 23, which is 6 pixels.
Hmm, but col 17 is the right edge of the blue block, and col 23 is the right edge of the white block. So the player moved from the right edge of one block to the right edge of the block 2 positions away.
I think the player really does move 2 cells (6 pixels) per action. But then my BFS should have hit a wall...
Wait, let me re-read the first batch result more carefully:
"executed_count": 35, "stopped_early": False
So all 35 actions were executed. But the player ended at [23, 40], not at the goal. Let me trace through the path with 2-cell movement:
Start: cell (0, 0)
1. RIGHT → (0, 2): W
2. RIGHT → (0, 4): W
3. DOWN → (2, 4): W
4. DOWN → (4, 4): W
5. DOWN → (6, 4): W
6. DOWN → (8, 4): W
7. RIGHT → (8, 6): W
8. RIGHT → (8, 8): W
9. UP → (6, 8): W
10. UP → (4, 8): B ← WALL!
But the actions continued past step 10. So either the wall check is different, or my grid is wrong.
Let me re-examine the grid. Looking at row 4, col 8:
Row 4: B B W g W B W B B B W
Col 8 is the 9th element (0-indexed): B
Hmm, that's a wall. But the player apparently passed through it.
Wait, maybe I'm reading the grid wrong. Let me re-extract it more carefully.
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
If I take the center pixel of each 3x3 block:
- Col 15-17: center col 16 → 'b' (from row 16)
- Col 18-20: center col 19 → 'g'
- Col 21-23: center col 22 → 'W'
- Col 24-26: center col 25 → 'B'
- Col 27-29: center col 28 → 'W'
- Col 30-32: center col 31 → 'g'
- Col 33-35: center col 34 → 'W'
- Col 36-38: center col 37 → 'g'
- Col 39-41: center col 40 → 'W'
- Col 42-44: center col 43 → 'B'
- Col 45-47: center col 46 → 'B'
So row 0 of the compressed grid is: b g W B W g W g W B B
That matches what I had. So cell (4, 8) should be... let me check row 4:
Row 27-29 (pixels): BBBBBBBWWWgggWWWBBBWWWBBBBBBBBBWWWB
Taking centers:
- Col 15-17: 'B'
- Col 18-20: 'B'  
- Col 21-23: 'W'
- Col 24-26: 'g'
- Col 27-29: 'W'
- Col 30-32: 'B'
- Col 33-35: 'W'
- Col 36-38: 'B'
- Col 39-41: 'B'
- Col 42-44: 'B'
- Col 45-47: 'W'
So row 4 is: B B W g W B W B B B W
Cell (4, 8) = B (wall)
But the player apparently moved through it. This is confusing.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the player moves 1 cell per action, and the initial move from [16,17] to [16,23] is because the player is at the right edge of the blue block and moves to the right edge of the NEXT block (which is 3 pixels away, to col 20), but then the display shows the player at col 23 because... no, that doesn't make sense.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the first move result:
"Charcoal nodes after RIGHT: [(9, [[16, 23]])]"
The charcoal node boundary is [[16, 23]], which is a single pixel at row 16, col 23. So the player moved from [16, 17] to [16, 23], a distance of 6 pixels.
Hmm, but what if the player actually moved to [16, 20] (the ggg block) and then the display is showing something different? Let me check the ascii after the first move...
Actually, I don't have the ascii after the first move anymore since the game reset. Let me try a different approach.
Let me just try moving RIGHT once from the current position and see where the player ends up. Then I can determine the movement pattern.
Actually, I already know the game is in a reset state. Let me just try a few test moves to understand the mechanics, then solve the maze properly.
But I'm already at 51 actions and over budget. Let me be more careful this time.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the maze is not a regular grid at all. Maybe the colored blocks form corridors and the player moves along them. The black blocks are walls.
Looking at the maze structure:
- The blue block is at (15-17, 15-17)
- Adjacent to it are: ggg at (15-17, 18-20) to the right, and BBB at (18-20, 15-17) below
- The ggg block connects to WWW at (15-17, 21-23) to the right
So the player starts at blue, and can move right through ggg to WWW, or down through BBB... but BBB is a wall.
Wait, maybe the player can only move through blocks of the SAME color family? Like, blue connects to gray connects to white, but not to black?
Or maybe the maze is a "corridor" maze where the colored blocks form paths and black blocks are walls. The player moves from one block to an adjacent non-black block.
Let me try this hypothesis: the player moves 1 block (3 pixels) per action, and can only move to adjacent non-black blocks.
From blue at (0,0), moving RIGHT goes to ggg at (0,1). But the player ended up at [16, 23] which is in the WWW block at (0,2). So this doesn't match either.
OK, I'm going in circles. Let me just do a simple test: move RIGHT once and see where the player goes. Then I can determine the movement pattern.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Game over reset the board. My BFS was wrong - need to understand the movement mechanics better. The player moved 6 pixels (2 cells) on the first RIGHT action, but my 2-cell BFS still failed. Need to test movement carefully.
Plan: Do a single test move to verify the movement pattern, then rebuild the maze model.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test single RIGHT move to understand mechanics
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
charcoal_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'c']
blue_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"Charcoal after RIGHT: {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in charcoal_nodes]}")
print(f"Blue after RIGHT: {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in blue_nodes]}")

# Get ascii around player to see what block they're in
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
# Check what's at row 16, cols 18-26
print(f"Row 16, cols 15-30: {lines[16][15:30]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the maze clearly. Let me look at the structure more carefully. The maze has alternating white (W) and gray (g) 3x3 blocks with black (B) separators. 
Looking at the pattern, I think the issue is that the maze might be a checkerboard pattern where only ONE color (either white OR gray) is walkable, not both. Let me think about this...
Looking at the maze:
- Row 0: bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
- Row 1: BBB BBB ggg BBB ggg BBB BBB BBB ggg BBB BBB
- Row 2: BBB BBB WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW
- ...
Wait, I see the pattern now. The maze has:
- 3x3 blocks of color (W, g, b, N)
- 3x3 blocks of B as walls between them
- But some adjacent blocks share edges directly
Let me look more carefully. The pattern seems to be:
- Each "cell" in the maze is 3x3 pixels
- Cells are separated by 3-pixel wide black corridors in some places
Actually, looking at row 15: `BbbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWWBBBBBBB`
This is: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
So the cells are:
- bbb (blue, player) at col 15-17
- ggg at col 18-20
- WWW at col 21-23
- BBB at col 24-26 (wall)
- WWW at col 27-29
- ggg at col 30-32
- WWW at col 33-35
- ggg at col 36-38
- WWW at col 39-41
- BBB at col 42-44 (wall)
- BBB at col 45-47 (wall)
- BBB at col 48-50 (wall)
So the maze is NOT a regular grid! Some cells are separated by BBB walls, and some are adjacent. The walkable path goes through alternating W and g blocks, with BBB blocks acting as walls.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the maze is a graph where:
- Each 3x3 non-black block is a node
- Adjacency is determined by whether two blocks share an edge
- The player moves between adjacent blocks
But wait, I already tried BFS treating all non-B cells as walkable and it failed. The issue might be that the player can only move through blocks of a specific color, or there's a different movement mechanic.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the first successful move (RIGHT from start):
- Player was at blue block (15-17, 15-17)
- After RIGHT, player moved to... let me check the transition
Actually, the first RIGHT move was successful - the player moved from [16,17] to [16,23]. That's a move of 6 columns, which is 2 cells (each cell is 3 pixels). Wait, no - [16,17] to [16,23] is 6 columns, but the blue block is at cols 15-17 and the next block (ggg) is at cols 18-20, then WWW at 21-23. So the player moved from the blue block to the WWW block, skipping the ggg block.
Hmm, that's strange. Let me re-examine. The charcoal was at [16,17] which is the rightmost column of the blue block. After RIGHT, it moved to [16,23] which is the rightmost column of the WWW block at cols 21-23. So the player jumped over the ggg block!
Wait, maybe the player moves 2 cells at a time? Or maybe the maze is structured differently than I thought.
Let me look at the structure again:
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
If the player is at the blue block and moves RIGHT, it goes to the WWW block (skipping ggg). This suggests the player moves through corridors, not through the colored blocks themselves.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the maze structure. Maybe:
- The colored 3x3 blocks (W, g, b, N) are the walls/obstacles
- The black 3x3 blocks (B) are the corridors/path
- The player moves through the black corridors
But that doesn't make sense either because the player starts on a blue block...
Let me look at this differently. Maybe the maze is made of 6x6 "super-cells" where each super-cell contains a pattern of 3x3 blocks. Or maybe the movement is different.
Actually, let me look at the first move more carefully. The player was at [16,17] (within blue block at 15-17, 15-17). After RIGHT, the player was at [16,23]. The distance is 6 columns. Looking at the ascii:
- Col 15-17: bbb (blue)
- Col 18-20: ggg (gray)  
- Col 21-23: WWW (white)
So the player moved from the blue block to the white block, passing through the gray block. The player seems to move 2 cells (6 pixels) per action.
Wait, but that means the player moves 2 blocks per action. Let me re-examine the compressed grid with this understanding.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the maze structure is:
- Each "cell" is actually 6x6 pixels (two 3x3 blocks)
- The player moves one cell (6 pixels) per action
Let me re-build the grid with 6x6 cells:
Row 15-20, Col 15-47:
- Cell (0,0): rows 15-20, cols 15-20 → bbb/ggg in top 3 rows, BBB/ggg in bottom 3 rows
Hmm, this doesn't work cleanly either.
Let me try a different approach. Let me trace the actual path the player took in the first few moves to understand the movement pattern.
From the history:
1. Start: charcoal at [16,17]
2. After RIGHT: charcoal at [16,23] (moved +6 cols)
3. Then I did RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT...
The player moved 6 pixels per action. So each action moves the player 6 pixels in the given direction. This means the effective cell size is 6x6, not 3x3.
Let me rebuild the maze with 6x6 cells:
- Rows 15-47, that's 33 rows / 3 = 11 cells... no, 33/6 = 5.5, doesn't divide evenly.
Hmm, let me reconsider. The maze spans rows 15-47 and cols 15-47. That's 33 pixels in each direction. 33/3 = 11 cells. But the player moves 6 pixels per action, which is 2 cells.
So the player moves 2 cells per action. This means in the 11x11 compressed grid, each action moves the player 2 cells.
Let me re-examine my first BFS. The path was:
RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, ...
With 2-cell movement, RIGHT moves from col 0 to col 2, then RIGHT again moves to col 4, etc.
Wait, but I said the first RIGHT moved the player from the blue block at col 15-17 to the white block at col 21-23. That's from cell col 0 to cell col 2 in the 3x3 grid. So yes, each action moves 2 cells.
Let me rebuild the BFS with this understanding:
- Grid is 11x11 (3x3 pixel blocks)
- Each action moves 2 cells in the given direction
- Player starts at cell (0, 0)
- Goal is at cell (10, 10)
Let me check: from (0,0), RIGHT goes to (0,2), RIGHT again goes to (0,4), etc.
But wait, my first BFS assumed 1-cell movement. That's why the path was wrong! Let me redo the BFS with 2-cell movement.
Actually, let me verify this hypothesis first. Let me check what happened after the first few moves.
From the first batch of actions (RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN):
Starting from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0, 2)
- RIGHT → (0, 4)
- DOWN → (2, 4)
- DOWN → (4, 4)
- DOWN → (6, 4)
- DOWN → (8, 4)
- RIGHT → (8, 6)
- RIGHT → (8, 8)
- UP → (6, 8)
- UP → (4, 8)
- RIGHT → (4, 10)
- RIGHT → (4, 12) - out of bounds? Grid is 11x11, so col 12 is out of bounds.
Hmm, that would cause an issue. Let me check the grid again.
Actually wait, let me look at the grid more carefully:
Row 0: W g W B W g W g W B B
Row 1: B B g B g B B B g B B
Row 2: B B W B W g W g W g W
...
With 2-cell movement, from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0, 2) → W (walkable)
- RIGHT → (0, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (2, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (4, 4) → W (walkable)
- DOWN → (6, 4) → g (walkable)
- DOWN → (8, 4) → g (walkable)
- RIGHT → (8, 6) → g (walkable)
- RIGHT → (8, 8) → W (walkable)
- UP → (6, 8) → W (walkable)
- UP → (4, 8) → B (wall!)
That's the problem! At step 10 (UP from (6,8) to (4,8)), the cell is B (black/wall). So the path hit a wall.
But wait, the game didn't report game_over until much later. Let me re-check...
Actually, looking at the result: the game executed 35 actions but stopped early at action 14 with game_over. The executed actions were: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT.
Wait, that's from the SECOND batch of actions, not the first. The first batch executed all 35 actions without game_over. Let me re-read...
First batch result: executed 35 actions, no game_over, but level not completed. Player ended at [23, 40].
Second batch result: executed 14 actions, game_over at action 14.
So the first batch of 35 actions completed but didn't reach the goal. The player was at [23, 40] which is cell (6, 9) in the 3x3 grid (row (23-15)/3 = 2.67, hmm that doesn't work cleanly).
Let me recalculate. If the player moves 6 pixels per action (2 cells), and started at cell (0,0):
After 35 actions, the player would be at some position. Let me trace through...
Actually, I think the issue is more fundamental. Let me reconsider whether the player really moves 2 cells per action, or if my initial grid mapping was wrong.
Let me check the first move again:
- Start: charcoal at [16, 17]
- After RIGHT: charcoal at [16, 23]
- Difference: 6 columns
The blue block is at cols 15-17. After RIGHT, the player is at col 23. The block at cols 21-23 is WWW (white). So the player moved from the blue block to the white block.
But looking at row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
Col 15-17: bbb
Col 18-20: ggg
Col 21-23: WWW
So the player moved from col 15-17 to col 21-23, which is 2 cells (6 pixels) to the right. This confirms 2-cell movement.
But wait, maybe the movement isn't always 2 cells. Maybe it depends on the maze structure. Let me think about this differently.
What if the maze is actually a graph where nodes are the colored blocks and edges connect adjacent blocks? The player moves from one block to an adjacent block in the given direction.
Looking at the structure:
Row 15: bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
The blue block (bbb) at col 15-17 is adjacent to ggg at col 18-20, which is adjacent to WWW at col 21-23. So from blue, moving RIGHT would go to ggg, then to WWW.
But the player moved from blue directly to WWW, skipping ggg. This suggests the player moves 2 blocks per action.
OK let me just try a completely different approach. Let me build the grid with 6x6 cells and see if it makes more sense.
Actually, I realize the issue. The maze might not have uniform cell sizes. Let me look at the structure more carefully.
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
Row 18: B BBB BBB ggg BBB ggg BBB BBB BBB ggg BBB BBB
These rows have different patterns. Row 15 has colored blocks (bbb, ggg, WWW), while row 18 has mostly BBB with some ggg.
This suggests the maze has a checkerboard-like structure where some areas are walkable corridors and others are walls.
Let me try yet another approach: treat the maze as a graph where each connected non-black region is a node, and the player moves between adjacent nodes.
Actually, I think the simplest explanation is that the player moves 6 pixels (2 cells of 3x3) per action. Let me build the grid with this understanding and redo BFS.
Grid with 3x3 cells (11x11):
Row 0: b g W B W g W g W B B
Row 1: B B g B g B B B g B B
Row 2: B B W B W g W g W g W
Row 3: B B g B g B g B B B g
Row 4: B B W g W B W B B B W
Row 5: B B B B B B g B B B g
Row 6: W g W g W g W B W g W
Row 7: g B g B B B B B B B B
Row 8: W g W g W g W B W g W
Row 9: B B g B B B g B g B g
Row 10: W g W B B B W g W B N
With 2-cell movement, from (0,0):
- RIGHT → (0,2): W ✓
- RIGHT → (0,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (2,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (4,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (6,4): W ✓
- DOWN → (8,4): W ✓
- RIGHT → (8,6): W ✓
- RIGHT → (8,8): W ✓
- UP → (6,8): W ✓
- UP → (4,8): B ✗ WALL!
So the 10th action (UP) would hit a wall. But the first batch executed 35 actions without game_over. This means either:
1. My grid is wrong
2. The player doesn't move 2 cells per action
3. The player can move through walls
Let me reconsider. Maybe the player moves 1 cell per action, and my initial grid mapping was wrong.
Let me check: if the player moves 1 cell (3 pixels) per action:
- Start at [16, 17] (within blue block at cols 15-17)
- After RIGHT: [16, 23] (within white block at cols 21-23)
- That's a move of 6 pixels, not 3.
Unless... the player moves to the CENTER of the next cell. The blue block is at cols 15-17, center is col 16. The white block is at cols 21-23, center is col 22. But the player was at col 17 and moved to col 23, which is 6 pixels.
Hmm, but col 17 is the right edge of the blue block, and col 23 is the right edge of the white block. So the player moved from the right edge of one block to the right edge of the block 2 positions away.
I think the player really does move 2 cells (6 pixels) per action. But then my BFS should have hit a wall...
Wait, let me re-read the first batch result more carefully:
"executed_count": 35, "stopped_early": False
So all 35 actions were executed. But the player ended at [23, 40], not at the goal. Let me trace through the path with 2-cell movement:
Start: cell (0, 0)
1. RIGHT → (0, 2): W
2. RIGHT → (0, 4): W
3. DOWN → (2, 4): W
4. DOWN → (4, 4): W
5. DOWN → (6, 4): W
6. DOWN → (8, 4): W
7. RIGHT → (8, 6): W
8. RIGHT → (8, 8): W
9. UP → (6, 8): W
10. UP → (4, 8): B ← WALL!
But the actions continued past step 10. So either the wall check is different, or my grid is wrong.
Let me re-examine the grid. Looking at row 4, col 8:
Row 4: B B W g W B W B B B W
Col 8 is the 9th element (0-indexed): B
Hmm, that's a wall. But the player apparently passed through it.
Wait, maybe I'm reading the grid wrong. Let me re-extract it more carefully.
Row 15: B bbb ggg WWW BBB WWW ggg WWW ggg WWW BBB BBB BBB
If I take the center pixel of each 3x3 block:
- Col 15-17: center col 16 → 'b' (from row 16)
- Col 18-20: center col 19 → 'g'
- Col 21-23: center col 22 → 'W'
- Col 24-26: center col 25 → 'B'
- Col 27-29: center col 28 → 'W'
- Col 30-32: center col 31 → 'g'
- Col 33-35: center col 34 → 'W'
- Col 36-38: center col 37 → 'g'
- Col 39-41: center col 40 → 'W'
- Col 42-44: center col 43 → 'B'
- Col 45-47: center col 46 → 'B'
So row 0 of the compressed grid is: b g W B W g W g W B B
That matches what I had. So cell (4, 8) should be... let me check row 4:
Row 27-29 (pixels): BBBBBBBWWWgggWWWBBBWWWBBBBBBBBBWWWB
Taking centers:
- Col 15-17: 'B'
- Col 18-20: 'B'  
- Col 21-23: 'W'
- Col 24-26: 'g'
- Col 27-29: 'W'
- Col 30-32: 'B'
- Col 33-35: 'W'
- Col 36-38: 'B'
- Col 39-41: 'B'
- Col 42-44: 'B'
- Col 45-47: 'W'
So row 4 is: B B W g W B W B B B W
Cell (4, 8) = B (wall)
But the player apparently moved through it. This is confusing.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the player moves 1 cell per action, and the initial move from [16,17] to [16,23] is because the player is at the right edge of the blue block and moves to the right edge of the NEXT block (which is 3 pixels away, to col 20), but then the display shows the player at col 23 because... no, that doesn't make sense.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the first move result:
"Charcoal nodes after RIGHT: [(9, [[16, 23]])]"
The charcoal node boundary is [[16, 23]], which is a single pixel at row 16, col 23. So the player moved from [16, 17] to [16, 23], a distance of 6 pixels.
Hmm, but what if the player actually moved to [16, 20] (the ggg block) and then the display is showing something different? Let me check the ascii after the first move...
Actually, I don't have the ascii after the first move anymore since the game reset. Let me try a different approach.
Let me just try moving RIGHT once from the current position and see where the player ends up. Then I can determine the movement pattern.
Actually, I already know the game is in a reset state. Let me just try a few test moves to understand the mechanics, then solve the maze properly.
But I'm already at 51 actions and over budget. Let me be more careful this time.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the maze is not a regular grid at all. Maybe the colored blocks form corridors and the player moves along them. The black blocks are walls.
Looking at the maze structure:
- The blue block is at (15-17, 15-17)
- Adjacent to it are: ggg at (15-17, 18-20) to the right, and BBB at (18-20, 15-17) below
- The ggg block connects to WWW at (15-17, 21-23) to the right
So the player starts at blue, and can move right through ggg to WWW, or down through BBB... but BBB is a wall.
Wait, maybe the player can only move through blocks of the SAME color family? Like, blue connects to gray connects to white, but not to black?
Or maybe the maze is a "corridor" maze where the colored blocks form paths and black blocks are walls. The player moves from one block to an adjacent non-black block.
Let me try this hypothesis: the player moves 1 block (3 pixels) per action, and can only move to adjacent non-black blocks.
From blue at (0,0), moving RIGHT goes to ggg at (0,1). But the player ended up at [16, 23] which is in the WWW block at (0,2). So this doesn't match either.
OK, I'm going in circles. Let me just do a simple test: move RIGHT once and see where the player goes. Then I can determine the movement pattern.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Game over reset the board. My BFS was wrong - need to understand the movement mechanics better. The player moved 6 pixels (2 cells) on the first RIGHT action, but my 2-cell BFS still failed. Need to test movement carefully.
Plan: Do a single test move to verify the movement pattern, then rebuild the maze model.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test single RIGHT move to understand mechanics
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
charcoal_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'c']
blue_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"Charcoal after RIGHT: {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in charcoal_nodes]}")
print(f"Blue after RIGHT: {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in blue_nodes]}")

# Get ascii around player to see what block they're in
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
# Check what's at row 16, cols 18-26
print(f"Row 16, cols 15-30: {lines[16][15:30]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
